excerpts.evidence: An alert gives people critical information they need right away. For example, an alert can tell people about a problem, warn them when their action might destroy data, and give them an opportunity to confirm a purchase or another important action they initiated.
excerpts.supportsChecklistItem: Verify that the alert gives people critical information they need right away, such as a problem, destructive consequence, purchase confirmation, or other important initiated action.

excerpts.evidence: Use alerts sparingly. Alerts give people important information, but they interrupt the current task to do so. Encourage people to pay attention to your alerts by making certain that each one offers only essential information and useful actions.
excerpts.supportsChecklistItem: Verify that alerts are used sparingly and include only essential information and useful actions.

excerpts.evidence: Avoid using an alert merely to provide information. People don't appreciate an interruption from an alert that's informative, but not actionable. If you need to provide only information, prefer finding an alternative way to communicate it within the relevant context.
excerpts.supportsChecklistItem: Verify that alerts are not used merely to provide non-actionable information. Use contextual, noninterruptive messaging when no immediate action is needed.

excerpts.evidence: Avoid displaying alerts for common, undoable actions, even when they're destructive. For example, you don't need to alert people about data loss every time they delete an email or file because they do so with the intention of discarding data, and they can undo the action.
excerpts.supportsChecklistItem: Verify that alerts are not shown for common destructive actions that people intentionally initiated and can undo.

excerpts.evidence: In all alert copy, be direct, and use a neutral, approachable tone. Alerts often describe problems and serious situations, so avoid being oblique or accusatory, or masking the severity of the issue.
excerpts.supportsChecklistItem: Verify that alert copy is direct, neutral, approachable, and clear about the severity of the situation without sounding accusatory.

excerpts.evidence: Write a title that clearly and succinctly describes the situation. You need to help people quickly understand the situation, so be complete and specific, without being verbose. As much as possible, describe what happened, the context in which it happened, and why. Avoid writing a title that doesn't convey useful information — like "Error" or "Error 329347 occurred" — but also avoid overly long titles that wrap to more than two lines.
excerpts.supportsChecklistItem: Verify that the alert title clearly and succinctly describes the situation, including what happened, context, and why when possible. Avoid vague titles like "Error" and avoid titles that wrap beyond two lines.

excerpts.evidence: Include informative text only if it adds value. If you need to add an informative message, keep it as short as possible, using complete sentences, sentence-style capitalization, and appropriate punctuation.
excerpts.supportsChecklistItem: Verify that informative text is included only when it adds value and is kept as short as possible.

excerpts.evidence: Avoid explaining alert buttons. If your alert text and button titles are clear, you don't need to explain what the buttons do.
excerpts.supportsChecklistItem: Verify that alert body text does not explain button behavior when the alert text and button titles are already clear.

excerpts.evidence: If supported, include a text field only if you need people's input to resolve the situation. For example, you might need to present a secure text field to receive a password.
excerpts.supportsChecklistItem: Verify that alerts include a text field only when user input is needed to resolve the situation.

excerpts.evidence: In all platforms, alerts display a title, optional informative text, and up to three buttons. On some platforms, alerts can include additional elements.
excerpts.supportsChecklistItem: Verify that alerts use no more than three buttons.

excerpts.evidence: Create succinct, logical button titles. Aim for a one- or two-word title that describes the result of selecting the button. Prefer verbs and verb phrases that relate directly to the alert text — for example, "View All," "Reply," or "Ignore." In informational alerts only, you can use "OK" for acceptance, avoiding "Yes" and "No." Always use "Cancel" to title a button that cancels the alert's action.
excerpts.supportsChecklistItem: Verify that alert button titles are succinct, logical, usually one or two words, and describe the result of selecting the button. Prefer verbs or verb phrases, use "Cancel" for cancellation, avoid "Yes" and "No," and reserve "OK" for purely informational alerts.

excerpts.evidence: Avoid using OK as the default button title unless the alert is purely informational. The meaning of "OK" can be unclear even in alerts that ask people to confirm that they want to do something. A specific button title like "Erase," "Convert," "Clear," or "Delete" helps people understand the action they're taking.
excerpts.supportsChecklistItem: Verify that "OK" is not used as the default button title unless the alert is purely informational. Use a specific action title like "Erase," "Convert," "Clear," or "Delete" when confirming an action.

excerpts.evidence: Place buttons where people expect. In general, place the button people are most likely to choose on the trailing side in a row of buttons or at the top in a stack of buttons. Always place the default button on the trailing side of a row or at the top of a stack. Cancel buttons are typically on the leading side of a row or at the bottom of a stack.
excerpts.supportsChecklistItem: Verify that the most likely or default button appears on the trailing side in a row or at the top in a stack, and that Cancel appears on the leading side in a row or at the bottom in a stack.

excerpts.evidence: Use the destructive style to identify a button that performs a destructive action people didn't deliberately choose. In contrast, people appreciate an alert that draws their attention to a button that can perform a destructive action they didn't originally intend.
excerpts.supportsChecklistItem: Verify that destructive style is used for destructive actions people did not deliberately choose, especially when the alert needs to draw attention to a destructive result they may not have intended.

excerpts.evidence: If there's a destructive action, include a Cancel button to give people a clear, safe way to avoid the action. Always use the title "Cancel" for a button that cancels an alert's action. Note that you don't want to make a Cancel button the default button.
excerpts.supportsChecklistItem: Verify that alerts with destructive actions include a Cancel button, titled "Cancel," and that Cancel is not the default button.

excerpts.evidence: Provide alternative ways to cancel an alert when it makes sense. In addition to choosing a Cancel button, people appreciate using keyboard shortcuts or other quick ways to cancel an onscreen alert. For example: Exit to the Home Screen on iOS and iPadOS; pressing Escape (Esc) or Command-Period (.) on an attached keyboard on iOS, iPadOS, macOS, and visionOS; pressing Menu on the remote on tvOS.
excerpts.supportsChecklistItem: Verify that alerts provide alternative cancellation methods when appropriate, such as Escape or Command-Period on supported platforms.

excerpts.evidence: When possible, avoid displaying an alert that scrolls. Although an alert might scroll if the text size is large enough, be sure to minimize the potential for scrolling by keeping alert titles short and including a brief message only when necessary.
excerpts.supportsChecklistItem: Verify that alerts avoid scrolling when possible by keeping titles short and including brief informative text only when necessary.
